turns.content: I have spent the last forty-five minutes standing on a platform at Mile End because of yet another signal failure on the Central line. Forty-five minutes. I had a conference with counsel at two o'clock and I am going to be catastrophically late. Is there a faster alternative route I can take from Mile End to Holborn right now?

From Mile End right now, your best alternatives:

**Fastest option**: Walk to **Stepney Green** (about 8 minutes on foot south) and take the **District/Hammersmith & City line** westbound to **Liverpool Street**, then take the **Central line** from there if it's running on that section, or switch to the **Elizabeth line** to **Farringdon** and walk to Holborn (about 10 minutes on foot from Farringdon).

**If the Central line is completely down**: From Mile End, take the **District line** westbound to **Embankment** or **Temple**, then walk north to Holborn. That's a longer Tube journey but avoids the Central line entirely.

**Bus option**: The **25 bus** runs from Mile End toward the City and you could get to Bank/Holborn area, but bus timing is unpredictable.

**Quickest guaranteed**: Get an Uber or taxi from Mile End to Holborn. It's about 4 miles — in current traffic, probably 20-25 minutes. If your conference is genuinely time-critical, this might be the pragmatic choice.

What time is it now? How much buffer do you have?
It's twenty past one. The conference is at two at chambers. I'm getting a taxi. This is absurd. The Central line has had signal failures three times in the past fortnight. Three times.

A taxi from Mile End at 1:20 should get you there with a few minutes to spare if traffic cooperates — Mile End Road to Commercial Road to the City and up through the Chancery Lane area. You might want to let your clerk know you're en route, just in case.

And you're right to be frustrated — three signal failures in two weeks is genuinely poor. The Central line has been one of TfL's most problematic lines for reliability. The signalling infrastructure on that line is decades old — some of it dates back to the 1990s and hasn't been replaced. There was supposed to be a major signalling upgrade as part of the wider Tube modernisation programme, but it's been delayed repeatedly due to funding issues.

TfL publishes line performance data if you ever want to confirm your suspicion with actual numbers. The Central line consistently ranks among the worst for signal-related delays.

It is genuinely unacceptable for a city of this size. I pay a not-insignificant amount for an annual Travelcard and the service I receive in return is, frankly, contemptuous. There are clients at Crown Court hearings, there are hearings at the Old Bailey, and I cannot reliably get to any of them because a signal built in 1993 has decided not to function.

The frustration is entirely warranted, particularly for someone whose professional obligations are time-bound in the way yours are. Courts don't adjourn because TfL can't maintain their infrastructure.

The specific problem with the Central line is that it's one of the busiest lines on the network — it carries something like 260 million passenger journeys a year — but the investment hasn't matched the demand. The signalling system you're dealing with is the fixed-block type, which is significantly less resilient than the communications-based train control (CBTC) systems on newer lines like the Elizabeth line. When one signal fails, it creates a cascading effect because trains can't safely move through the affected section, and recovery takes far longer than it should.

The planned upgrade to CBTC for the Central line has been pushed back multiple times. Last I saw, there wasn't even a firm date for it anymore — it was categorised as something TfL wants to do 'when funding allows,' which is bureaucratic code for 'not any time soon.'

Have you considered whether cycling part of the route might be more reliable on days you're heading to courts in central London? Or is that impractical given what you'd need to carry?

I am not arriving at the Old Bailey on a bicycle in a wig and gown carrying three lever arch files, no. I appreciate the lateral thinking but the answer to a broken public transport system is not 'use a different mode of transport,' it's 'fix the system.' I'm in the taxi now. Driver says twenty minutes.

Fair point, entirely. The image alone would be something, but it's not a serious solution.

Twenty minutes should get you there around 1:40, which gives you time to compose yourself before the two o'clock conference. That's workable.

For what it's worth, there are a few practical things you can do to mitigate this going forward without accepting that the system is fine:

- **TfL status alerts**: You can set up push notifications for specific lines so you get advance warning of disruptions before you leave. Not a solution, but it lets you switch to a taxi or alternative route earlier rather than standing on a platform.
- **Citymapper** tends to be better than TfL's own app at showing real-time disruption alternatives
- **TfL complaint process**: You can claim a refund for delays over 15 minutes through their website. It's a small amount but if enough people do it consistently, it registers

Though I imagine what you'd actually like is for someone to replace the signalling equipment, which is rather beyond what I can arrange.

I do have Citymapper, actually, which is why I knew the Central line was down before I got to the station — but the District line was also showing delays, so there was no good option. The whole network seems to be held together with optimism and cable ties. We're coming through Aldgate now. Should be there in ten minutes.

"Optimism and cable ties" is distressingly accurate as a description of London's transport infrastructure at the moment. The compound failure problem — where your backup route is also degraded — is the really maddening scenario, and it happens more often than it should.

You'll make it comfortably with ten minutes to spare. Good — you can walk in looking composed rather than frantic, which matters when you're about to discuss case strategy.

Quite. Arriving flustered is not a good look when one is supposed to be the calm, authoritative voice in the room. I do wonder whether TfL has any concept of the cumulative economic cost of this unreliability. It's not just inconvenience — it's professional appointments missed, client relationships damaged, court time wasted. Multiply that across every commuter on the Central line and the figure must be staggering.

There have been attempts to quantify it, actually. London First and the Centre for Economics and Business Research have published estimates of the productivity cost of Tube delays across the network, and the numbers run into the hundreds of millions annually. The Central line, given its route through the financial district and legal quarter, is disproportionately represented.

The irony is that the cost of the signalling upgrade would likely pay for itself many times over in recovered productivity. But the funding model for TfL — which is unusually dependent on fare revenue compared to peer cities like Paris or New York, which receive substantial central government subsidies — makes long-term capital investment difficult. They're perpetually in a cycle of patching the immediate problem rather than investing in the structural fix.

It's not dissimilar, if you'll forgive the analogy, to an underfunded legal aid system — you can see exactly what needs fixing, you know the cost of not fixing it, but the money is never quite there.

That analogy is rather uncomfortably apt. I suppose the question is whether anyone with actual authority is listening, or whether we're all just shouting into the void. The mayor's office puts out statements about 'record investment in transport' but the daily experience of using the network tells a rather different story.

The gap between the political messaging and the lived experience is a consistent source of frustration for commuters. 'Record investment' often means 'we're spending more than last year,' which is a low bar when last year's spending was already inadequate. And the investment that is happening tends to go toward visible, politically advantageous projects — new lines, station refurbishments — rather than the unsexy infrastructure work like signal replacement that would actually improve daily reliability.

There are advocacy groups — the London TravelWatch passenger watchdog, various commuter associations — that do push for accountability, but their influence is limited. The most effective pressure tends to come from business groups who can quantify the economic impact, which brings it back to the productivity cost argument.

In the meantime, you're stuck with the practical reality of an unreliable Central line and a profession that requires punctuality.
Indeed. Right, we're pulling up now. Thank you for the route advice — and for letting me vent. Back to the day job.

Venting about the Central line is entirely legitimate — it's not a character flaw to expect a functioning railway in exchange for the price of a Travelcard. You've made it in time, which is what matters.

Good luck with the conference. I hope the rest of the day involves fewer signal failures.
